title: A Spectral Gap Informed Parameter Schedule for QAOA
authors: Kieran McDowall, Konstantinos Georgopoulos, Petros Wallden
abstract: A challenge with the Quantum Approximate Optimisation Algorithm (QAOA), and variational algorithms in general, is finding good variational parameters, a task which in itself can be NP-hard. Recent work has sought to de-variationalise QAOA by picking well-informed guesses for the variational parameters. The Linear Ramp QAOA (LR-QAOA) achieves this by using parameter schedules inspired by the quantum adiabatic algorithm. We go a step further and use spectral gap information from an adiabatic Hamiltonian, with the QAOA mixer Hamiltonian as our initial Hamiltonian, to make smooth ramps which we call Spectral Gap Informed Ramps (SGIR-QAOA). SGIR-QAOA schedules perform slow evolution where the spectral gap of the adiabatic Hamiltonian is small. We show that SGIR-QAOA has performance improvements over LR-QAOA on Grover's problem at constant depth and that SGIR-QAOA requires shorter depths to achieve the same optimal solution probability. We then show that these performance benefits extend to a problem with potential practical applications -- the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem. Finally, we demonstrate the scalability of the SGIR-QAOA method using extrapolated spectral gap information for scales that the spectral gap cannot be exactly evaluated, and show that the advantage appears to persist under mild depolarising noise.
summary: This paper introduces a new parameter scheduling strategy for the QAOA algorithm called SGIR-QAOA. By using information about the spectral gap of an adiabatic Hamiltonian, the algorithm slows down its evolution during periods where the gap is small, effectively mitigating diabatic transitions. The authors demonstrate that this approach provides superior performance and better scaling for both Grover's search and the Maximum Independent Set problem compared to standard linear ramp methods. Furthermore, the method proves to be scalable through gap extrapolation and remains effective even in the presence of depolarizing noise.
main_problem: Finding optimal variational parameters for the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) is computationally difficult, and existing 'de-variationalized' schedules like the Linear Ramp (LR-QAOA) fail to account for the closing of the spectral gap.
main_result: The proposed Spectral Gap Informed Ramp (SGIR-QAOA) outperforms LR-QAOA on Grover's problem and the Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problem, requiring shallower depths to reach target success probabilities and demonstrating better exponential scaling.
method: The authors develop a parameter schedule that uses smooth ramps informed by the spectral gap of an adiabatic Hamiltonian, performing slower evolution where the gap is small, and use extrapolation techniques to scale the method to larger problem sizes.
model_or_system: The study utilizes the QAOA framework with alternating mixer and cost Hamiltonians, specifically testing on Grover's search problem, Erdős–Rényi random graphs, and 3-regular sparse graphs for the Maximum Independent Set problem.
key_observables: Optimal solution probability (Ps), required QAOA depth (p), and the minimum spectral gap (gmin).
important_parameters: QAOA depth (p), problem size (n), spectral gap (g), and depolarizing noise strength.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that the spectral gap information from smaller system sizes can be accurately extrapolated to larger system sizes where exact calculation is impossible.
figures_summary: Figures show the eigenvalue spectrum of Grover's problem, comparisons of success probability (Ps) across different schedules, plots of required depth (p) vs. problem size (n), and the correlation between performance improvement and the minimum spectral gap.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of parameter optimization in QAOA, presents the SGIR-QAOA method, validates it using Grover's problem, extends the analysis to combinatorial optimization (MIS) on various graph types, demonstrates scalability via extrapolation, and tests robustness against depolarizing noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. (Top) Eigenvalue spectrum from HAd for Grover’s problem with the marked solution state |x⟩= |000000⟩(n = 6). (Bottom) The optimal Roland Cerf (RC) adiabatic schedule.
Fig. 2. (Top) Plotting Equation 7 for Grover’s problem k = 0 n = 4, with the marked solution state |x⟩= |0000⟩discritised by p = 10. (Bottom) An example of the corrsponding SGIR schedule.
Fig. 4. Solving Grover’s problem where the QAOA depth p required to reach an optimal solution threshold P th s at different problem sizes is plotted.

Fig. 3. Solving Grover’s problem with our QAOA methods at constant QAOA depth p = 10. At each n the experiment is repeated 10 times with a different random marked solution. The random QAOA parameters are changed for each of these instances. The y-axis is logarithmic.

Fig. 5. Solving MIS degree 3 graphs with LR–QAOA and exact SGIR–QAOA. At each n the experiment is repeated 10 times with a different randomly generated instance. A log scale is used on the y-axis. The error in the fit for the exponential scaling coefficient is included in the legend.
Fig. 6. Solving the MIS problem with degree 3 graphs where the QAOA depth p required to reach an optimal solution threshold P th s at different problem sizes is plotted.

Fig. 8. Solving the MIS problem (degree three graphs) under depolarising noise. Averaging over 10 instances at the 10 node problem size. λ = 100, searching 20×20 (βstart, γend) and (∆γ, ∆β) values for each p. The shaded region correspond to the standard deviation error of the noisy points. In Figure 11 we plot the noiseless LR–QAOA and SGIR–QAOA showing behaviour at larger p .

Fig. 7. Solving MIS degree 3 graphs with extrapolated SGIR–QAOA. The gaps for n = 6 −12 are calculated which inform the SGIR– QAOA schedule for n > 12. At each n the experiment is repeated 10 times with a different randomly generated instance. A log scale is used on the y-axis. The error in the fit for the exponential scaling coefficient is included in the legend.

Fig. 10. Solving MIS degree 3 graphs with extrapolated SGIR–QAOA at larger n than in Figure 7. The gaps for n = 6 −12 are calculated which inform the SGIR–QAOA schedule for n > 12. At each n the experiment is repeated 10 times with a different randomly generated instance. A log scale is used on the y-axis. The error in the fit for the exponential scaling coefficient is included in the legend.
Fig. 9. The percentage improvement of SGIR-QAOA against LR- QAOA, p = 10, with the minimum spectral gap of the problem (Pearson correlation coefficient ρ = −0.68).
